title: Bergamot and cedarwood reed diffuser
subtitle: A room diffuser blend with bergamot, cedarwood, and dipropylene glycol carrier
excerpt: Dipropylene glycol (DPG) is the standard carrier for reed diffusers. It draws through the rattan reeds by capillary action and evaporates slowly, carrying the fragrance blend into the room. Bergamot is the top note; cedarwood anchors the base. The blend suits a living room or hallway.
sourceNotes: Reed diffuser formulation from Gracefruit fragrance-making guide (gracefruit.com, May 2026). DPG properties from supplier datasheet. Bergamot EO safety from Essential Oil Safety, Tisserand and Young (2014).

recipe.batchNotes: Scale proportionally. Each 100 ml diffuser lasts approximately 4 to 6 weeks.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make in advance and pour into the diffuser bottle when ready. The blend improves with a few days of maceration.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: 12 months sealed, away from direct sunlight. Once the reeds are inserted, expect 4 to 6 weeks of active diffusion. Flip the reeds every 2 to 3 days to re-saturate and boost scent output.

recipeTools.notes: Or a small jug for blending and pouring

glossaryTerms.definition: A synthetic liquid carrier with very low odour, used in reed diffusers to thin fragrance oils and essential oils for wicking. DPG is less viscous than pure essential oil, which allows it to draw up rattan reeds by capillary action. It extends the life of the diffuser by slowing evaporation compared with a pure EO blend.

Turning the reeds over in the diffuser bottle to expose the dry end to the liquid. Over time, the liquid wicks towards the tip and the base of the reed becomes dry. Flipping re-saturates the dry end and produces an immediate boost in scent output. Flip every 2 to 3 days.

content.text: Reed diffusers depend on capillary action: the natural tendency of liquids to move through narrow channels against gravity. Porous rattan reeds draw the

blend upward through the reed and release it from the tip by evaporation. Placing the diffuser in a spot with gentle air movement: near a door, a vent, or a window: speeds up diffusion. Still air gives a subtler, more constant release.

Use bergapten-free (FCF) bergamot essential oil. Cold-pressed bergamot is phototoxic and can leave marks on skin if the diffuser mist contacts the skin. FCF bergamot has the phototoxic compound (bergapten) removed and is safe for diffuser use in a living space.
every 2 to 3 days to maintain output.

items.prepNote: The carrier; thins the blend for wick travel through the reeds
12% of formula; use FCF (bergapten-free) bergamot; the top note
8% of formula; the base note; grounds the bergamot
Standard rattan diffuser reeds; 8 reeds for a 100 ml bottle

content.text: Combine the DPG and both essential oils in a small jug and stir to blend. Pour into a clean narrow-necked diffuser bottle. Insert the reeds. Place on a stable surface away from direct sunlight, which fades the bergamot top note faster. Flip the reeds immediately after inserting to saturate the tips and start diffusion. Flip every 2 to 3 days thereafter.

The sealed blend keeps for 12 months. Once the reeds are inserted, 4 to 6 weeks of active diffusion. Top up with fresh blend when the bottle is nearly empty, using fresh reeds: old reeds become saturated and clogged and diffuse less effectively.
